content.body: Chemical reactions involve breaking bonds (requires energy) and forming bonds (releases energy). Whether a reaction overall releases or absorbs energy determines whether it is exothermic or endothermic. This is studied in thermochemistry.

Exothermic reactions: release energy (heat) to surroundings
• Temperature of surroundings INCREASES
• Products have LESS energy than reactants
• ΔH (enthalpy change) is NEGATIVE: ΔH < 0
• Examples: combustion, oxidation, neutralisation, respiration

Endothermic reactions: absorb energy from surroundings
• Temperature of surroundings DECREASES
• Products have MORE energy than reactants
• ΔH is POSITIVE: ΔH > 0
• Examples: thermal decomposition, photosynthesis, dissolving ammonium nitrate

Standard Enthalpy Changes (ΔH°):
ΔH°combustion: enthalpy change when 1 mole burns completely in O₂
ΔH°formation: enthalpy change when 1 mole formed from elements
ΔH°neutralisation: enthalpy change when acid + base → 1 mol water

Hess's Law: enthalpy change of a reaction is independent of the route
ΔH°reaction = Σ ΔH°(products) - Σ ΔH°(reactants)
or
ΔH°reaction = Σ ΔH°f(products) - Σ ΔH°f(reactants)

content.title: Bond Energy Calculations
content.body:
Energy to BREAK bonds = positive (endothermic)
Energy released FORMING bonds = negative (exothermic)

ΔH = energy to break bonds - energy released forming bonds

Example: H₂ + Cl₂ → 2HCl
Bonds broken: H–H (436 kJ) + Cl–Cl (243 kJ) = 679 kJ
Bonds formed: 2 × H–Cl (431 kJ each) = 862 kJ
ΔH = 679 - 862 = -183 kJ/mol (exothermic ✓)

Energy level diagrams:
Exothermic: products lower than reactants on energy diagram. 'Hill' shows activation energy.
Endothermic: products higher than reactants.

Activation energy (Ea): minimum energy for reaction to occur.
Catalyst: lowers Ea → faster reaction at same temperature.
On energy diagram: lower 'hill' with catalyst.

questions.question: An exothermic reaction has ΔH:
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release heat (energy flows out). ΔH is negative (ΔH < 0).

questions.question: Combustion of fuel is:
questions.explanation: Combustion releases large amounts of heat energy — strongly exothermic.

questions.question: Breaking chemical bonds:
questions.explanation: Bond breaking always requires energy input (endothermic). Bond forming releases energy.

questions.question: Hess's Law states:
options: Enthalpy change depends on the reaction route
Enthalpy change is INDEPENDENT of the route taken
questions.answer: Enthalpy change is INDEPENDENT of the route taken
questions.explanation: Hess's Law: the total enthalpy change is the same regardless of how many steps or which route the reaction takes.

questions.question: Photosynthesis is endothermic because:
options: Energy (light) is absorbed to form glucose from CO₂ and H₂O
questions.answer: Energy (light) is absorbed to form glucose from CO₂ and H₂O
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis absorbs light energy — endothermic. Products have more energy than reactants.

questions.question: Bonds broken = 400 kJ, bonds formed = 550 kJ. ΔH = ?
questions.explanation: ΔH = energy in − energy out = 400 − 550 = −150 kJ. Exothermic.

questions.question: A catalyst affects a reaction by:
questions.explanation: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. ΔH (enthalpy change) is unchanged.

questions.question: ΔH°neutralisation is the heat change when:
options: 1 mole of water forms from acid and alkali
questions.answer: 1 mole of water forms from acid and alkali
questions.explanation: Standard enthalpy of neutralisation: energy change when acid and base react to form exactly 1 mole of water.

questions.question: In an energy level diagram for an endothermic reaction:
options: Products are at the same level
questions.explanation: Endothermic: energy absorbed, so products end up at a HIGHER energy level than reactants.

questions.question: Which always has ΔH = 0?
options: Formation of elements in standard state
questions.answer: Formation of elements in standard state
questions.explanation: By definition, the standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its standard state is zero.

lessons.title: Chemical Equilibrium and Le Chatelier's Principle

content.body: Many chemical reactions are reversible — products can react to re-form reactants. In a closed system, a reversible reaction eventually reaches dynamic equilibrium: the rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal and concentrations remain constant (but not necessarily equal).

Reversible reaction notation: A + B ⇌ C + D

At equilibrium:
• Concentrations of all species are constant (but not equal)
• Both forward and reverse reactions still occur
• The system appears static but is dynamically balanced

Equilibrium constant Kc:
For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD:
Kc = [C]c[D]d / [A]a[B]b

Large Kc: equilibrium lies to the RIGHT (mostly products)
Small Kc: equilibrium lies to the LEFT (mostly reactants)

Le Chatelier's Principle:
'If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change, the system will shift to oppose that change and restore equilibrium.'

EFFECT OF CHANGES:
↑ Concentration of reactant → shifts RIGHT (makes more products)
↑ Concentration of product → shifts LEFT
↑ Temperature → shifts in the ENDOTHERMIC direction
↓ Temperature → shifts in the EXOTHERMIC direction
↑ Pressure (gases) → shifts toward FEWER MOLES of gas
Catalyst: no effect on position — only reaches equilibrium faster

content.title: The Haber Process — Industrial Application
content.body:
N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g)   ΔH = -92 kJ/mol (exothermic)

For maximum ammonia yield:
• High pressure (4 moles gas → 2 moles gas: shift right)
• Low temperature (exothermic: shift right, more NH₃)
• BUT low temperature → very slow rate!

Compromise conditions used:
Temperature: ~450°C (compromise: reasonable rate AND yield)
Pressure: 150-300 atm
Catalyst: iron (to increase rate without affecting equilibrium)

This is why industrial chemistry always balances yield vs rate vs cost!

questions.question: Dynamic equilibrium means:
options: Forward and reverse rates are equal; concentrations constant
questions.answer: Forward and reverse rates are equal; concentrations constant
questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium: both reactions still occur, but at equal rates, so no net change in concentrations.

questions.question: Le Chatelier's Principle predicts that adding more reactant to equilibrium will:
questions.explanation: Adding reactant increases its concentration. System opposes this by consuming reactant — shifting right to make more products.

questions.question: For exothermic forward reaction, increasing temperature:
options: Shifts equilibrium to the left (less product)
questions.answer: Shifts equilibrium to the left (less product)
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium in the endothermic direction (reverse reaction) — to absorb the added heat. Less product.

questions.question: N₂+3H₂⇌2NH₃. Increasing pressure shifts equilibrium:
questions.explanation: Left has 4 mol gas, right has 2 mol. Higher pressure favours fewer gas moles — shift right → more NH₃.

questions.question: A catalyst affects equilibrium by:
options: Not changing position — only reaching equilibrium faster
questions.answer: Not changing position — only reaching equilibrium faster
questions.explanation: A catalyst lowers activation energy of BOTH forward and reverse reactions equally. It speeds up reaching equilibrium but doesn't change Kc.

questions.question: Kc = [NH₃]² / ([N₂][H₂]³). Large Kc means:
options: Equal amounts of products and reactants
questions.explanation: Large Kc: product concentrations large relative to reactant concentrations — equilibrium lies to the right.

questions.question: Haber process compromise temperature (~450°C) is used because:
options: Low temp gives good yield but too slow; 450°C balances rate and yield
questions.answer: Low temp gives good yield but too slow; 450°C balances rate and yield
questions.explanation: Lower temperature gives more NH₃ (exothermic equilibrium) but very slow rate. 450°C is the industrial compromise for economic efficiency.

questions.question: Removing a product from an equilibrium mixture:
questions.explanation: Removing product decreases its concentration. System opposes this by producing more product — shift right.

questions.question: In the Haber process, the iron catalyst:
options: Allows faster attainment of equilibrium without changing yield
questions.answer: Allows faster attainment of equilibrium without changing yield
questions.explanation: Iron catalyst increases the rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally — equilibrium is reached faster but the yield at equilibrium is unchanged.

questions.question: Which change shifts N₂O₄⇌2NO₂ to the RIGHT?
options: Adding N₂O₄ and then removing NO₂
questions.answer: Adding N₂O₄ and then removing NO₂
questions.explanation: Adding N₂O₄ (reactant) and removing NO₂ (product) both shift equilibrium to the right — system tries to restore balance.

content.body: Redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons between species. Redox is short for Reduction-Oxidation. These reactions power batteries, drive metabolism, and are used in the extraction and refining of metals.

Definitions:
Oxidation: LOSS of electrons (or gain of oxygen, or loss of hydrogen)
Reduction: GAIN of electrons (or loss of oxygen, or gain of hydrogen)
Oxidising agent: causes oxidation in others — gets REDUCED itself
Reducing agent: causes reduction in others — gets OXIDISED itself

Oxidation state rules:
• Free element: oxidation state = 0
• Monatomic ion: = charge on ion
• O usually = −2 (except peroxides: −1)
• H usually = +1 (except metal hydrides: −1)
• Sum of oxidation states in neutral molecule = 0
• Sum in ion = charge of ion

content.title: Assigning Oxidation States
content.body:
Find oxidation state of Cr in K₂Cr₂O₇:
K is +1. O is -2. Let Cr = x.
2(+1) + 2x + 7(-2) = 0
2 + 2x - 14 = 0
2x = 12 → x = +6
Cr is in +6 oxidation state.

Find oxidation state of Mn in MnO₄⁻:
Let Mn = x. O = -2. Charge = -1.
x + 4(-2) = -1
x - 8 = -1 → x = +7

content.title: Half-Equations and Full Ionic Equations
content.body:
Oxidation half-equation: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
Reduction half-equation: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu

Full ionic equation: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu

Balancing half-equations:
1. Balance atoms (use H₂O for O, H⁺ for H in acid)
2. Balance charge (add e⁻)
3. Combine half-equations, cancelling electrons

questions.question: Oxidation is:
questions.explanation: OIL: Oxidation Is Loss of electrons.

questions.question: In Zn→Zn²⁺+2e⁻, zinc is:
questions.explanation: Zinc loses 2 electrons — it is oxidised. Loss of electrons = oxidation.

questions.question: The reducing agent:
options: Gets oxidised and causes reduction in another substance
Is the same as the oxidising agent
questions.answer: Gets oxidised and causes reduction in another substance
questions.explanation: The reducing agent donates electrons (gets oxidised) and causes the other substance to be reduced.

questions.question: Oxidation state of O in H₂O:
questions.explanation: O is -2 in most compounds (except peroxides). H=+1, O=-2. Sum: 2(+1)+(-2)=0. ✓

questions.question: Oxidation state of S in SO₄²⁻ (O=-2):
Oxidation state of Fe in Fe₂O₃:
Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. What is oxidised?
questions.explanation: Mg goes from 0 to +2 — loses electrons — oxidised. H goes from +1 to 0 — gains electrons — reduced.

questions.question: In the reaction 2Fe³⁺ + Zn → 2Fe²⁺ + Zn²⁺, what is the oxidising agent?
questions.explanation: Fe³⁺ gains electrons (is reduced) and causes Zn to be oxidised — Fe³⁺ is the oxidising agent.

questions.question: Disproportionation means:
options: Same element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced
questions.answer: Same element is simultaneously oxidised and reduced
questions.explanation: Disproportionation: one element in the same species undergoes both oxidation and reduction simultaneously.

questions.question: Balancing half-equations: MnO₄⁻→Mn²⁺ in acid. Add:
questions.explanation: MnO₄⁻ + 8H⁺ + 5e⁻ → Mn²⁺ + 4H₂O. Mn goes from +7 to +2 (gains 5e⁻). 4O balanced with 8H⁺ → 4H₂O.
